title.value: Barriers and enabling factors for error analysis in NLG research
title.evidence: Barriers and enabling factors for error analysis in NLG research

year.notes: Assumed based on the latest references in the paper.

abstract.value: Earlier research has shown that few studies in Natural Language Generation (NLG) evaluate their system outputs using an error analysis, despite known limitations of automatic evaluation metrics and human ratings. This position paper takes the stance that error analyses should be encouraged, and discusses several ways to do so. This paper is based on our shared experience as authors as well as a survey we distributed as a means of public consultation. We provide an overview of existing barriers to carrying out error analyses, and propose changes to improve error reporting in the NLG literature.
abstract.evidence: Earlier research has shown that few studies in Natural Language Generation (NLG) evaluate their system outputs using an error analysis, despite known limitations of automatic evaluation metrics and human ratings. This position paper takes the stance that error analyses should be encouraged, and discusses several ways to do so. This paper is based on our shared experience as authors as well as a survey we distributed as a means of public consultation. We provide an overview of existing barriers to carrying out error analyses, and propose changes to improve error reporting in the NLG literature.

doi.notes: No DOI provided in the text.

venue.value: International Conference on Natural Language Generation (INLG)
venue.notes: Assumed from context as it discusses NLG.

problem_statement.value: Despite the usefulness of error analyses, relatively few NLG papers included them, indicating a significant gap in the evaluation practices of NLG research.
problem_statement.evidence: Despite the usefulness of error analyses, Van Miltenburg et al. (2021a) have shown in their survey of INLG papers published in 2010, 2015, and 2020 that relatively few NLG papers included them (about 11% of the papers surveyed).

value: Identify barriers to conducting error analyses in NLG research.
Encourage the inclusion of error analyses in NLG literature.
Propose changes to improve error reporting practices.
research_objectives.evidence: We aim to provide clarity on both counts. Based on earlier work by Van Miltenburg et al. (2021a) and our own experiences as NLG researchers, we identified nine different factors that might influence authors in their decision (not) to carry out an error analysis.

survey_description.value: A survey was conducted among NLG researchers to gather opinions on error analysis and identify barriers and enabling factors for conducting such analyses.
survey_description.evidence: We carried out a public consultation in the form of a survey among NLG researchers to ask for their opinions on error analysis and to identify additional barriers and enabling factors for carrying out an error analysis.

number_of_participants.evidence: We received 72 responses (consenting to the survey and answering at least one question).

value: Academia
Industry
Other
affiliations.evidence: Of those who indicated their affiliation, 51 were academics, eight were from industry, and one selected 'other'.

data_analysis.value: A quantitative analysis of closed questions and a qualitative analysis of open responses were performed to identify themes related to barriers and enabling factors for error analyses.
data_analysis.evidence: We performed a quantitative analysis of the responses to our closed questions. In addition, we performed a qualitative analysis of the open question responses, inspired by other qualitative approaches, such as thematic analysis.

value: Most NLG researchers appreciate error analyses but face barriers such as time and resource constraints.
Many respondents indicated that a culture change towards error analysis is necessary to increase its adoption.
findings.evidence: Our findings suggest that NLG researchers generally appreciate error analyses they see in the work of others, but they are held back from carrying out an error analysis themselves for various reasons.

value: Lack of time and resources.
Absence of established error taxonomies.
Insufficient recognition from peers and reviewers.
barriers_identified.evidence: Participants confirmed that resources are the premier barrier: time (including the time that could be allocated for improving the NLG systems), funds, tools to help with error analyses including a taxonomy of errors.

value: Increased recognition and rewards for error analyses in publication processes.
Development of standard methodologies and resources for error analysis.
enabling_factors.evidence: A number of participants mentioned that the current research culture does not reward such analyses, which prevents them from performing and reporting them.

implications.value: The findings suggest that fostering a culture that values error analysis could improve the robustness of NLG research and its outputs.
implications.evidence: Our proposals should be seen as part of the broader and ongoing discussions on publication and peer review in NLP.

value: Develop tools and resources for error analysis.
Promote structural changes in publication incentives to encourage error analysis.
future_work.evidence: In the future, we would like to focus on developing tools and resources, such as error taxonomies, annotation tools, and clear guidelines that would help to encourage more routine and robust error analyses.

irb_approval.evidence: Before carrying out our study, we obtained ethical approval from the Institutional Review Board (IRB) at the lead author’s university.

participant_rights.value: Participants were informed of their rights, including the ability to withdraw consent at any time without negative consequences.
participant_rights.evidence: Your participation is completely voluntary, and you have the right to decline to participate and withdraw from the research once participation has begun, without any negative consequences.

data_availability.value: The code and data for this research project are freely available online.
data_availability.evidence: The code and data for this research project are freely available online.

methodological_transparency.value: The survey methodology and results are documented and available for review.
methodological_transparency.evidence: We made sure to analyse our responses before the deployment of our survey, and committed the report to GitHub, so that it would be time-stamped.
